title: Quiz: Chemical Process Optimization
description: Test your understanding of computational process optimization

questions.question: If the activation energy difference between two pathways is 2 kcal/mol, what is the approximate selectivity at 25°C?
questions.explanation: Selectivity S = exp(-ΔΔEa/RT). With ΔΔEa = 2 kcal/mol and RT = 0.592 kcal/mol at 298K: S = exp(2/0.592) = exp(3.38) ≈ 30. A 2 kcal/mol barrier difference gives ~30:1 selectivity at room temperature.

questions.question: Why are polar aprotic solvents preferred for SN2 reactions?

options.text: They solvate cations but leave anions 'naked' for attack

questions.explanation: Polar aprotic solvents like DMSO and DMF solvate cations well but don't hydrogen-bond to anions. This leaves the nucleophilic anion 'naked' and more reactive, accelerating SN2 attack. Protic solvents would H-bond to and deactivate the nucleophile.

questions.question: Which solvation model is best for screening many solvents quickly?

options.text: QM/MM with hundreds of water molecules

questions.explanation: COSMO-RS uses statistical thermodynamics to predict solvation properties for any solvent from a single DFT calculation of the solute. It's fast enough to screen 50-100 solvents in hours, while explicit solvation would take days per solvent.

questions.question: What is a key indicator of potential thermal runaway hazard?
questions.explanation: Adiabatic temperature rise (ΔT_ad = -ΔH_rxn/mCp) indicates how much temperature would increase if all reaction heat were retained. ΔT_ad > 100K suggests significant thermal hazard and potential for runaway if cooling fails.

questions.question: Computational predictions of side reactions can replace the need for experimental impurity profiling.
questions.explanation: False. Computational predictions guide where to look for impurities and suggest mechanisms, but regulatory requirements demand experimental identification and quantification of impurities. Computation and experiment work together.

questions.question: Which information can DFT provide for process development? (Select all)
questions.explanation: DFT can calculate mechanisms, predict temperature effects on selectivity through activation energies, and identify potential side reactions by comparing pathway barriers. Production cost involves many non-chemical factors (labor, equipment, utilities) outside DFT's scope.
